trajectory_summary: The call is a fast, structurally thin booking. Jake moved from intro to appointment in under four minutes on a warm inbound, which is efficient, but the speed came at the cost of qualification depth, bill anchoring, and any curiosity creation. The appointment is booked but fragile — no bill captured, no spouse/title co-owner check completed, no emotional anchor established, and a credit threshold error that may not have been confirmed by Joseph at all.
outcome_dispute: v1 tag is unknown. The observable outcome is a booked in-person appointment for the following day at 2:30. However, the booking is structurally fragile: bill not captured, co-owner status unknown, credit confirmation ambiguous, and Joseph's 'Can I verify?' at [01:44] went unaddressed. A booked-but-fragile tag would be more accurate than a clean 'appointment set.'

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Title co-owner assumption — spouse protocol gap
primary_coaching_focus.quote: In order to qualify for all of them, there's two things you need. One is to be on the title of your house, which I assume is no issue for you.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Jake assumes sole ownership without asking, skipping the codex-required title co-owner check. On a call this short, this is the single gap most likely to produce a no-show or a 'I need to talk to my wife' deflection at the closer stage. It is also the easiest fix: one question, asked naturally, before the credit qualifier. The manager should replay this moment and have Jake practice the title question until it is automatic.

key_moments.quote: 760. Is that the case for you? Sorry. 670. My apologies.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jake states the credit threshold as 760, catches himself, and corrects to 670. The codex threshold is 670; a prior call (SESSION18_fcbf33c1) recorded a rep stating 650. This is the third data point on threshold drift. More critically, the affirmations at [02:45] — 'Yes. Yeah. Yes. Okay. Okay. Cool. Cool. Cool.' — appear to be Jake's voice, not Joseph's. If that read is correct, Joseph never verbally confirmed he meets the credit threshold. The closer may be walking into a proposal with an unverified qualifier.

Joseph asks 'Can I verify?' and receives no response — Jake's next line at [01:51] is 'How how long have you lived in, this house for?' The question is completely unaddressed. It is unclear what Joseph wanted to verify: the company's identity, the address Jake read back, or something else. This is an unresolved customer utterance that leaves a trust gap entering the appointment.

key_moments.quote: Oh, I'm like, I just moved to the this house there now. Oh, you did? The first month.
key_moments.stewart_read: Joseph is a brand-new homeowner — moved in December, first month of bills. This is an enthusiasm signal, not a disqualifier. New homeowners are setting up their home's cost structure from scratch; the timing to lock in energy costs before Dominion's rates compound over years is a genuine value anchor. Jake hears it as a potential complication ('Oh, you did?') and moves past it without building on it.

key_moments.quote: We do have a couple different, like, kinda, like, pricing or financing options.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jake volunteers that Ion has 'a couple different pricing or financing options' without being asked. This is mild scope creep — the setter's job is to qualify and schedule, not to preview the closer's product menu. It does not derail the call, but it opens a door to pricing questions Jake is not equipped to answer at setter stage.
